# Compliant Control

## Why Position-Only Policies Are Not Enough

- Position commands alone do not specify interaction behavior.
- The same motion can be safe or unsafe depending on stiffness.
- Contact-rich manipulation needs motion and mechanical response.

TODO: Insert contact force illustration at `figures/position_only_limitations.png`.

---

## Position, Force, and Compliant Control

- Position control: track desired motion.
- Force control: regulate interaction force.
- Compliant control: shape the dynamic relation between force and motion.

---

## Impedance Control Intuition

Impedance control defines how the robot should generate forces in response to motion errors.

Useful mental model:

- high stiffness: precise but less forgiving
- low stiffness: safer but less accurate
- damping: dissipates energy and smooths response

---

## Admittance Control Intuition

Admittance control maps measured external force to a motion correction.

This is useful when the low-level robot interface accepts position or velocity commands.

---

## Mass-Spring-Damper Equation

A common admittance model is:

$$
M_d \ddot{x}_r + D_d \dot{x}_r + K_d x_r = F_{ext}
$$

where $M_d$, $D_d$, and $K_d$ are desired mass, damping, and stiffness.

---

## Variable Stiffness and Damping

Instead of fixed gains, the controller can adapt:

$$
K_d = K_d(t, o_t),
\qquad
D_d = D_d(t, o_t)
$$

This creates task-dependent compliance.

---

## Stability and Safety Intuition

- Learned parameters must remain within safe bounds.
- Fast changes in stiffness or damping can destabilize interaction.
- Passivity and energy-based reasoning provide useful guardrails.

TODO: Insert safety envelope figure at `figures/compliance_safety_bounds.png`.
